Yes, exactly, just exactly six months. Although it felt quite long subjectively, I myself felt time passed too quickly when I was in Russia. At that time, I went to San Pietroburg, and also visited several other nearby cities in the region, traveling around as a tourist.

Then I met many different strangers—singers, but they were all extremely friendly. For example, we got to know some Russians. We might only have encountered them through news or the internet before, but when you actually have a face-to-face conversation with a foreigner, it's completely different. Yet their common trait was their kindness—they showed me genuine goodwill. As a result, I had a very enjoyable six months there.

Some, for example, when I mentioned to my classmates how incredibly nice a certain person was, he immediately said "love at first sight" and expressed his feelings openly. Also, what you just mentioned—social shock—on the other hand, reinforces my stereotypes about them. One thing is that they really love alcohol. Then this "goodnight circle" is just the same as my personal impression of Russia. At that time, I was thinking that perhaps they—after all, I had only interacted with a part of Russians—had many more people who couldn't be summed up by a single statement like "Russians."

But it's precisely because they've gone to such extremes that they develop such thoughts. Many of my classmates are the same— their goals are all set by their parents. They have no personal interests, no independent ideas; everything they think is derived from their parents, who tell them, "You must get into a top school."

It means that after becoming a postgraduate, you should either pass the entrance exam or get through the interview process to enter a top company, and then advance to a top position. You mentioned earlier that we've touched upon this: the very purpose of exchange programs is to explore more possibilities—both the unknown and our own potential. Now, if you plan to study abroad after graduation, have you thought about your future career? Personally, I've considered this, and one reason I've ruled out studying abroad is that my family, partner, and friends are all in China. I'm someone who deeply values close relationships, so I'm reluctant to go abroad because it would be too far, especially with the time difference. Daily life would be completely out of sync, and I'd miss being close to the people I care about. Therefore, I hope that after completing my graduate studies, I can still maintain a fulfilling personal life through my work.

I fully understand what you just mentioned about translation, because actually our environments are quite similar—both of us are engaged in language-related studies and, broadly speaking, foreign communication. Regarding translation, I myself am quite interested in it and have had some experience, and I find that the current state of translation is quite inadequate. Even when I watch films—both domestic and foreign—I pay attention to the translation aspect. Many Chinese elements are currently rendered only in pinyin form. However, I personally view the presence of certain pinyin terms as a form of cultural export. For instance, the term "dragon" is now being actively promoted abroad not as "dragon" but as "loong," reflecting an effort to convey the distinct cultural essence of the Chinese concept.

Actually, I first started working out in Russia. At that time, our school had its own gym, and since I was relatively quiet and there were fewer people around, I felt uneasy and thus went to the gym less often.

Then, although I usually go swimming, because our school happens to have a swimming pool—though not very large—it's just right for someone who wants to exercise, and I've stayed quite fit as a result. However, swimming actually involves quite a few steps; it takes time to shower and get ready, making it less convenient. So I don't go as often as I'd like, but I still cherish every opportunity to exercise, because motivation must always come from within. For example, I initially thought two hours between classes wouldn't be enough, but time always seems to be found if you make an effort. So I try my best to fit it in. That's why I even bought a swimming pass.

I'm so sorry about your bicycle. I completely understand. But as you said, it's actually not strictly regulated by equipment standards—since, for example, regular shared bicycles can also be ridden. Still, I wish you all the best and hope you can get your new bicycle as soon as possible.

Because the city where I lived in Russia is called New Petrograd, it's a relatively modern city with exceptional inclusiveness. It hosts a wide variety of communities, including a large number of Chinese residents who are well distributed throughout the city. As a result, the people there are generally more open-minded and very kind to us, so I personally experience this issue less frequently. However, I completely agree with what you just said: the development of a city or of the world as a whole should not be based solely on the interests of a small group.

Then, the most heartbreaking part is that, right among us, there are such so-called vulnerable groups—our younger siblings, tiny babies, or elderly grandparents—who lack the ability, the capacity, to protect themselves. As a result, they inevitably become offerings, even products, of global development—sacrifices, so to speak. I hope I can do my utmost to stop this trend.

I strongly, strongly, strongly agree. Because, as you just mentioned, simply going out is already a very serious issue for them. There are so many obstacles on the way, making it extremely difficult. By the way, we should show more compassion and empathy toward them. They just want to leave their homes, yet they still have to figure out how to deal with problems—such as how to face obstacles on the way, or how they're unaware that they are invisible people. These are all very significant challenges.
I feel that if you're busy with language studies, how do you usually relax in your daily life?
You just said you often miss home—does your family have any pets, like dogs or cats?

Sure, sure. I often go to Cinema to watch movies, but you just mentioned your home is in Makao, which means your place is actually in Seaside. In Beijing, there are no rivers or seas, so it's really dull. For example, a few days ago I went to Tianjin—we drove for an hour and a half to get there—and there was actually a sea. Whenever I go to Seaside, I can enjoy sunrises and sunsets, which feels truly healing. In Beijing, you simply don’t have such opportunities. Speaking of pets, I think I’m gradually turning our home into a zoo. Since I’ve always loved animals, I’ve always been fond of cats, dogs, flowers, and plants. So now we have two cats, a dog, and a rabbit—the rabbit is the newest addition.

Because I happened to have a neighbor whose family just welcomed a newborn baby, and the baby seemed to have a slight allergy to rabbit fur, so the neighbor asked in the group chat if anyone wanted a baby rabbit. We responded, "Oh, how cute! He's really so cute!" Then we said we could take care of it for a while. We were truly surprised to find that the cat and the rabbit didn't fight at all—they actually got along quite harmoniously.

You mentioned that, and it reminds me of last semester in the UK, as I lived near Manchester. Since Manchester is close to the west coast, I often took the train to a place called Blackpool—actually just Blackpool City, a seaside resort. The name "Blackpool" comes from the many rocks along the coast, so the seawater often appears black. But when I visited, I didn't really find it that dark. What struck me was how incredibly numerous the seagulls were. Everywhere I looked, there were stickers on the railings—local people's humorous stickers saying things like "Seagulls, please stop screaming!" Whenever I went there, I'd always bring some bread or snacks; the seagulls would frequently fly over and snatch fries right from our hands.

There are so many fish and chips over there, and seagulls absolutely love them. I remember that whenever I felt completely overwhelmed while writing my thesis, I'd take a train, head to the seaside, watch the seagulls, enjoy the sunset, and occasionally toss them a few chips. It always made me feel happy and relaxed. I think Beijing is a city with an extremely fast pace of life—everyone seems to be rushing around, as if constantly chasing trains or planes. That’s how it feels every single day.

Yes, I fully understand—what you said is very insightful and logical. But you just mentioned you have study-abroad experience. So when you went to Russia, since I've also traveled there, I assume your stay must have been longer than mine. How long were you there? About six months?

Have you ever experienced culture shock? Were there any aspects that you found difficult to adapt to, or things that surprised you because they were completely different from what you understood? Or did you manage to break any stereotypes?

I felt I experienced a great deal of culture shock in the UK as well. For example, my first impression of British people was that they are extremely polite, gentle, and kind—though to some extent this is true, and to some extent it isn't. Some people, especially certain Iranians or Germans, speak languages you simply can't understand. Likewise, some British or German people speak in a way that you can't comprehend at all. They're not speaking standard English; they're using some unfamiliar form of English. Sometimes even native British people don't understand it.

I do think there's indeed an issue with this, but I feel that for many people, this is their destiny. Because, to a large extent, education can truly serve as the key to transforming one's fate. My boyfriend, during his vacation, brought along his close friend to Beijing. His friend comes from a rural area in Hunan.

Then, I think especially for some rural areas or regions with less developed economies, the college entrance exam sometimes really is, as that female teacher from the girls' high school once said, the only path to truly changing one's life. Only by gaining admission to a good college or university can one possibly have the opportunity to engage with academic circles or international matters.

Because there are many classmates around me from the Property area, sometimes when I interact with them, they feel that simply being born in Beijing, or in a big city, already puts you ahead of at least one generation. It's truly striking how severe the educational disparities caused by wealth gaps have become. Yet I believe this is also an international issue.

Because in our translation class we deal with a wide variety of materials, there was once a session when a Venezuelan teacher showed us a tech talk. The speaker was the founder of Duolingo, who is apparently also Venezuelan. He explained why he created Duolingo—a free app—specifically to enable more people to learn English. He mentioned that in some foreign countries, one might have to pay dozens or even hundreds of dollars just to attend a single class.

Then there are the wealthy individuals. For example, in the United States, some attend private schools, gradually advancing to prestigious institutions like Harvard or Yale. However, individuals from less affluent backgrounds may lack the resources to access better educational opportunities, and as a result, they often end up attending church schools or community colleges.

It feels like financial issues bring about educational challenges. But I think what you said makes a lot of sense. If we want to change this situation, it's quite important to re-examine the original purpose of education. Nowadays, there are many initiatives, for example, in Beijing, various resources from primary schools are being evaluated and redistributed. Some underperforming schools are being balanced out by sharing resources with other schools. For instance, teaching assistants are being assigned to provide weekly instruction and guidance. Additionally, better-qualified younger teachers and teenage teachers are being sent to rural areas to teach.

I think what you said is absolutely correct. I believe that although there has been constant advocacy in the workplace for greater consideration toward women—how we should treat them, and so on—such practices have actually always existed. For example, I often feel that many women, when seeking jobs, are not explicitly told by employers that "you're not the right person" or anything similar.

But they all have considerations, especially many of them. Recently, we had a teacher who was going to give birth. But we, all the students, truly felt that she was going through immense hardship. We were shocked to learn that someone who was eight to nine months pregnant still came to work.

My goodness, it was his huge belly—since we were studying language, the class was mostly made up of older female students, with just one male student. But that guy was quite considerate, so we all thought, "My goodness, is he going to give birth any second now?" We were all extremely worried during every class, yet at the same time felt this workplace truly wasn't suitable.

Then we felt that he really worked extremely hard every day. Sometimes he would stand up to give us classes; sometimes he'd just wander around, stand for a while, sit for a while, and so on. I thought this was truly exhausting. On the other hand, many employers and employees often think that since you're a woman, you're expected to get married, have a baby, give birth, and then suddenly drop out of a project, making it impossible to keep up. Or, during an important meeting, you suddenly become pregnant and need to go through morning sickness.

It feels like they have many concerns, but I think what's needed is a connecting effort. They should recognize that having such a lifestyle and living in such an environment is normal for us. At the same time, they shouldn't say, "Because you're a woman, I won't give you this opportunity." I know from previous job postings in companies that when it comes to living alone and managing everything independently, they often tell women, "You can't handle this burden," and simply say no, implying that women can't endure such hardships.

Moreover, I frequently saw small blue signs in the UK indicating that hearing-impaired individuals could place their headphones there to hear amplified sound through a larger speaker. I rarely see such facilities in China.
